# Heft & Pattillo (Group Jazz), Engaging Everyone with Liberating Structures Handbook (deep reference)

**Source:** Lisa Heft & Anne Pattillo (compilers), with materials by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless (2010). *Engaging Everyone with Liberating Structures Handbook*. Washington, DC: Group Jazz. URL: https://www.grassrootsgrantmakers.org/wp-content/uploads/Engaging-Everyone-31.pdf. Licence: **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported (CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 Unported)**.
**Scope:** open-nc
**Structure:** 60-page handbook with no chapter numbering. Sections (in source order): a copyright/introduction page; a Table of Contents; an Introduction defining liberating structures and the "jazz" metaphor; seven framing sections (Ownership versus Buy-in; Choosing Space; Aesthetics Are Important; Working with the Space You're Given; Storyboard Design and Planning; Nothing About Me Without Me; Engage the Unusual Suspects); a "LIBERATING STRUCTURES" catalogue of roughly 32 named interaction designs (Knee-to-Knee Conversation; One Minute of Silence; Celebrity Interviews; Taking Questions; Take 10 (Minutes); Take a Panel; A Note about Report Outs; Wise Crowds; Participatory Scenarios; Six Words; Just In Time Topics; 5 Whys; Impromptu Speed Networking; 1-2-4-Whole Group; Appreciative Interviews; Discovery & Action Dialogues; TRIZ; Wicked Questions; 15% Solutions; Conversation Café Dialogue; World Café; Troika Consulting; Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix; 25-to-10; After Action Debrief: What, So What, Now What?; Gallery Walk; Mini Constellations; Levels of Accountability; Fishbowl; Network Weaving; Q Storming; Simple Rules; Shift & Share; Portfolio Planning; Improv Games); a "Seeing Complexity and Complex Systems" group with three exercises (Complex Systems Game: How Many Ways?; Tic Tac Toe; A Place in the System); a "Random Thoughts" group (Waving Hands versus Clapping; Talking Objects; Music & Poetry; Tingsha Bells; Take 20 Seconds); an "Origins" section attributing the term to Torbert (1991) and de Bono (1991); and a closing "A Pattern Language for Engagement" essay drawing on Alexander (1977) and the Johnson-Lenz primitives.
**Citation style:** `(Section, "Sub-section name")`. The handbook is unnumbered; each named structure is its own sub-section header. Page-number markers (`2`, `3`, …, `60`) are preserved in the converted markdown but not used in citations because sub-section names are more navigable than the 1-or-2-page spans they bracket.
**Coverage:** complete — all 60 pages of the source were deep-read. Image classification was deferred (text-only ingest); the handbook contains small illustrative line drawings and diagrams (a few page-corner sketches; an Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix diagram on p. 36; a "Levels of Accountability" stair-step diagram on p. 41; a Participatory Scenarios 2×2 on p. 22). None carry conceptual content not also present in the text.

## Author's thesis (paraphrased from source)

Liberating structures are "frameworks that make it possible for people and organizations to create, to do new things, to be innovative. These are processes or rules that can be put in place to encourage people to be free, creative, and get results, rather than find themselves oppressed, constrained, confined, or powerless" [V] (Introduction). The handbook's thesis is structural rather than motivational: "For things to really change, structural elements need to change, too. Otherwise change is short-lived. Liberating structures are the forms that make it easy for people to be generative together and make a significant impact with their creativity" [V] (Introduction). The designs that work share two paradoxical qualities — they are "messy" and "complex", producing dialogue that "feels generative", yet at the same time they have "just enough structure to channel the energy and keep things moving and productive. These structures are liberating rather than confining" [V] (Introduction).

The handbook's central metaphor is jazz: "Through its underlying structure, people are able to play together. In fact, people who have never seen each other, never before met, can sit down and jam. They can create something that is wonderful. The guidelines of jazz are a collection of principles that give enough structure so that people can create together. These same principles make possible infinite degrees of freedom" [V] (Introduction). The compilers' working hypothesis is that engagement-design can be made similarly accessible — that "the power to host and facilitate high engagement" can be put "in the hands of everyone in the organization" if the patterns are named and shared [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement).

A second load-bearing claim is the *ownership versus buy-in* distinction: "Ownership is when you own or share the ownership of an idea, a decision, an action plan, a choice. It means that you have participated in its development; that it is your choice freely made" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in); "Buy-in is the exact opposite. Someone else, or some group of people, has done the development, the thinking and the deciding, and now they have to convince you to come along and buy-in to their idea -- so that you can implement their idea without your involvement in the initial conversations or resulting decisions. Aiming for buy-in creates lukewarm, pallid implementation and mediocre results" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in). The whole catalogue is presented as instruments for ownership: "Liberating Structures help you create true ownership and avoid the pitfalls of buy-in. When it comes to solving intractable socio-technical behavioral problems in systems the notion of buy-in is just not useful – people in the system need to own the new behaviors" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in). The handbook flags the word *buy-in* itself as a diagnostic warning: "Anytime you or someone around you thinks or talks about buy-in, beware! It is a danger signal telling you that your development and implementation process is missing the essential ingredient of involving all who should be involved" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in).

A third load-bearing claim is the *pattern-language* framing — that liberating structures are best understood as named, combinable patterns drawn from a small set of primitives. "As a pattern language for engagement, liberating structures give us multiple options for each of these primitives: the rhythm/timing of each round of interaction, the boundaries of group size and inclusion, physical containers like space and room set-up, and conceptual containers created by the way a question is phrased. All liberating structures are made up of these simple sets of components that can be combined in dozens of different ways" [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement). The handbook explicitly positions itself as "a helpful reminder, not complete instructions" for people who have attended an LS-based event [V] (front matter), making it a participant-facing reference for the patterns rather than a stand-alone instructional manual.

## Part I: Framing principles before any structure is used

### Ownership versus buy-in

The single most-cited framing contrast in the handbook. Ownership is participation in development; buy-in is being convinced after the fact to implement someone else's decision [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in). The handbook treats *buy-in* not just as a weaker alternative but as a structural error symptom: "Liberating Structures are all about creating ownership" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in). The diagnostic move is to listen for the word in conversation: hearing it is "a danger signal telling you that your development and implementation process is missing the essential ingredient of involving all who should be involved" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in). This framing — that consultative styles which sound participatory ("we'll bring it to them for buy-in") are in fact symptoms of insufficient up-front involvement — runs through every interaction design that follows.

### Choosing space, aesthetics, and working with what you have

Three sections name space as a load-bearing variable for engagement. "Many meetings and events work well with NO TABLES in the room" — chairs need to move easily, and "SMALL tables are much better than big tables" because "the round tables typically available in most conference centers and hotels are too large for juicy conversation because people end up too far apart and have to yell across to be heard in a full room" [V] (Choosing Space). Across all configurations, "you need lots of elbow room so it's easy to move around" [V] (Choosing Space).

Aesthetics are treated as similarly structural rather than decorative: "Lighting is critical and most people wilt when meeting all day in artificial – especially fluorescent - light. Choose a room with natural light if at all possible!" [V] (Aesthetics Are Important). The same section calls for colour ("Flowers and other natural materials can breathe life into a space and make it feel more welcoming") and graphic-recording surfaces ("It's great to have a big, long wall you can cover with blank paper for graphic recording") [V] (Aesthetics Are Important).

When the space cannot be chosen, the discipline is to bend the situation: "You can use Liberating Structures in any space. Of course, some spaces are more conducive to interaction than others" [V] (Working with the Space You're Given). Even rows of fixed chairs admit work-arounds: "Invite people to stand up, turn around, and talk to the person in the row behind. Invite people to stand up and make a group of three to have a conversation" [V] (Working with the Space You're Given).

### Storyboard design and planning

The handbook recommends planning meetings via storyboard rather than agenda: "Try using a storyboard instead of an agenda to design and plan a meeting, event, or even an initiative that will play out over time" [V] (Storyboard Design and Planning). The storyboard's defining feature is that "Unlike an agenda, it also includes and illustrates something about what the participants will experience in the course of the meeting" [V] (Storyboard Design and Planning). The diagnostic value is in the visible shape: "if you find your storyboard has a whole lot of podium sessions in a row, it's not going to be very engaging!" [V] (Storyboard Design and Planning). One operational technique is named: "use post-it notes to represent different components of the meeting so you can move them around to see how different combinations might feel" [V] (Storyboard Design and Planning).

### Nothing about me without me

A framing principle the handbook applies to positively-deviant solution-search: "When searching in your organization or community for behavior and solutions that are unusually powerful and effective (positively deviant), it helps to broaden your conversations to include the people most involved. This is especially powerful when you can bring together people that don't normally talk with one another" [V] (Nothing About Me Without Me). The handbook gives two worked examples in which a complaint about another group ("supplies never brings enough gloves"; "doctors need to round on MRSA-positive patients last") becomes a prompt for inviting the absent party: "Who could we talk with in supplies that might be able to help us fix this?"; "Which doctors could we ask about whether this makes sense?" [V] (Nothing About Me Without Me). The principle is presented as upstream of design: "Thinking about the Unusual Suspects is an important part of creating an invitation list for any gathering you design" [V] (Nothing About Me Without Me).

### Engage the unusual suspects

The companion principle to "Nothing About Me Without Me", framed as a single question: "Who's not here that could contribute to our conversation?" [V] (Engage the Unusual Suspects). The handbook calls this "one of the most important questions you can ask" [V] (Engage the Unusual Suspects). One caveat is named directly — the invitation craft matters because "Many people have been to too many meetings where they were invited as window dressing without the opportunity to contribute, so they may be suspicious" [V] (Engage the Unusual Suspects).

## Part II: The Liberating Structures catalogue (selected interaction designs)

The handbook's largest section is a catalogue of roughly 32 named LS interaction designs, each on one or two pages. The deep reference summarises each by its handbook framing; it does not import language or context from other LS publications. Where the handbook itself cites external lineage (a researcher, a tradition, a related practice), the lineage is recorded as a borrowed-through ([BT]) item.

### Conversation-starting and opening structures

**Knee-to-Knee Conversation.** "Peter Block talks about the power of small groups sitting knee to-knee - without the mediating effect of a table. Whenever possible invite participants to move closer together to create more intimacy" [V] (Knee-to-Knee Conversation) [BT — Peter Block; pointer to www.asmallgroup.net]. Operationally: "Set up the chairs in small 'knee-to-knee' groups before participants arrive. As they come in, they will naturally end up in small groups" [V]. Suggested opening questions are surfaced as paraphrasable starters: "What drew you to this meeting? What are you most excited about in your work right now? What are you bringing to this community? What audacious goal could we have?" [V] (Knee-to-Knee Conversation).

**One Minute of Silence.** "Give participants a full minute of silence to reflect on a question before starting an exercise" [V]. The framing is inclusive rather than introvert-specific: "A minute of reflection is not just for the introverts in your group. Everyone benefits from having a moment to think about something before jumping into a discussion" [V] (One Minute of Silence). The handbook offers a specific facilitator script that names the time-stretch effect: "I'm giving you a full minute to think about this before we start and I'm going to time it so it's EXACTLY a minute. For some of you, this minute will go by in a flash. For others, it will seem like it's taking forever. But I promise, it will be exactly one minute" [V] (One Minute of Silence). The debrief move: "invite participants to notice what the minute of silence contributed to the process if they don't bring it up themselves" [V].

**Impromptu Speed Networking.** "A great way to generate energy at the beginning of a meeting. It provides an opportunity for everyone to speak early" [V] (Impromptu Speed Networking). Operationally: provocative question, ring a bell, find a partner you know less well, 5–10 minute conversation, ring again, find another partner. "Three 'rounds' are usually good" [V]. The handbook names a load-bearing observation: "everyone gets to speak early in the meeting. The distance between not having said anything and making a first comment is vast. But the distance between having said something and saying another thing is much smaller" [V] (Impromptu Speed Networking). Arithmetic example: "In a 30-minute session, everyone will have had 12-15 minutes of air time – no matter how many participants are in the meeting!" [V].

**1-2-4-Whole Group.** A four-stage scaling structure: individual reflection ("a couple of minutes is fine"), pairs (5–10 minutes), small groups of 4–6 ("More than six is too big to give everyone a chance to talk"), then whole group with an open question such as "What insights emerged from your conversation?" or "How has your understanding/view of the issue changed?" [V] (1-2-4-Whole Group). The handbook notes the structure "can be done on its own or combined with Impromptu Speed Networking" [V].

### Surfacing-knowledge and reflection structures

**Celebrity Interviews.** A replacement for the conventional panel: "create a 'talk show' atmosphere where an interviewer can draw out catalysts in a more conversational way" [V] (Celebrity Interviews). The framing is functional — to "Convey a small number of ideas associated with a key dimension of a topic or project in a compelling, memorable, engaging way" and to "Avoid draining/deadening the energy in the meeting, which happens when people have to listen to any one voice for more than 5 minutes" [V] (Celebrity Interviews). The handbook gives a concrete example of question framing: "'What does the marketing data say?' tends to elicit boring responses. A better question might be, 'Having explored the marketing data, what surprised you? What strikes you as most important?'" [V] (Celebrity Interviews).

**Taking Questions.** The defining move is to take questions in batches: "If you invite participants to ask questions or share comments after Celebrity Interviews or other exercises, have participants speak in batches (3-4 at a time) rather than 1 by 1" [V] (Taking Questions). The reasoning is structural: "This avoids a problem that can happen when the first question sends the conversation off in a weird direction" and "avoids getting into a Q A Q A Q A Q A pattern that tends to reinforce the 'central tendency' versus the distributed intelligence of the group" [V] (Taking Questions).

**Take 10 (Minutes).** "Giving participants a chunk of time to think on their own about something can generate tremendous benefits and allow the conversation to go deeper than it otherwise might" [V] (Take 10 (Minutes)). Suggested prompts include: "How has your thinking changed about [blank] since we started this morning?", "What have you heard that's really got your juices flowing?", "What's the question you still need to answer for yourself to get to a significantly new place? Get unstuck?", and "What's been missing from our conversation so far?" [V] (Take 10 (Minutes)).

**Take a Panel.** Each participant has their own flip-chart sheet on the wall; given 8–10 generative questions and about 20 minutes, each person fills their sheet with words, drawings, or mind-maps. Each sheet must "stand alone — that can be understood without explanation by someone who comes by" [V] (Take A Panel). Then groups of 2–3 share with ~4 minutes each, asking "questions for clarification only" [V]. The handbook notes "This can work well as a 'soft start' activity for a meeting so people who arrive early or right on time can get busy rather than waiting for others to arrive" [V].

**A Note About "Report Outs".** Possibly the bluntest section in the handbook: "'Report Outs' from breakout sessions are BORING!!!" [V] (A Note about Report Outs). The handbook's framing is that report-outs typically "drain the life out of whatever conversation that took place in the small group" and that "It is more effective to ask participants to bring the experience they had in previous conversations into a new conversation. The important stuff will turn up again as part of a new thinking integration relevant to the next question or discussion framework" [V] (A Note about Report Outs). If documentation is needed, the handbook prescribes engagement with the participants rather than facilitator assumptions: "engage participants in a conversation about who actually needs this material and what might be the best and most useful way for them to get it. This, of course, works better if they can speak for themselves rather than have us make assumptions about them" [V] (A Note about Report Outs).

**Gallery Walk.** A named alternative to report-outs: "Have groups or teams put whatever they want to share on a poster… Everyone else takes a walk through the gallery, stopping at each poster to take in what's there" [V] (Gallery Walk). Followed by a small-group conversation: "What did we notice? What were the themes? What was surprising? What was new? How does this change our conversation?" [V].

### Working-with-experts structures

**Wise Crowds.** Groups of 5–6, sitting knee-to-knee, with one "client" and the rest as a "consulting team" [V] (Wise Crowds). Three structured phases. (1) Client describes a challenge for about 10 minutes; "the Consulting Team can ask a few questions for clarification, but should resist the temptation to go into major 'data gathering' mode" [V]. (2) The Client turns their chair around (back to the group) and listens silently while the consulting team converses about what might help — questions, paraphrases, metaphors, examples, musings — for at least 15 minutes; the team's "job is not to decide 'this is what The Client ought to do'" and the client "tries to resist the temptation to use body language and non-verbal utterances to convey what they think about what the Consulting Team is saying, but rather stay in open listening mode" [V] (Wise Crowds). (3) Client turns chair back and opens with "Here's what I heard that intrigued, surprised, or resonated with me" then free conversation for ~15 minutes [V].

**Troika Consulting.** Groups of three knee-to-knee. Each member gets a turn (10 minutes) as the focal person sharing their challenge; "the partners… first to ask questions to help them hone and improve their ideas. Next, the partners engage with each other and, finally, with the participant about how they might handle the challenge and what possibilities might contribute to moving forward" [V] (Troika Consulting). After all three rounds, "the group can spend some time in conversation about insights and patterns they noticed across the three rounds" [V]. Suggested framing questions include: "Think about a challenge you are facing in your work. How might you apply some of the ideas from this workshop?"; "What's the question you most need to answer in order to move forward?"; "What's the biggest obstacle to making the changes you want to make? What can you do now to move beyond that obstacle?" [V] (Troika Consulting).

**Appreciative Interviews.** Stories about "a time when…" something worked: "a time when our relationship with customers really worked; a time when I was able to suggest an out-of-the-box idea and got a positive response; a time when collaboration between our teams was successful" [V] (Appreciative Interviews). The handbook surfaces named characteristics of the practice: "Connection Through Empathy; Personal Excitement, Commitment, Care; Intense Focus Through 'Third Ear' and 'Third Eye'; Generative Questioning, Cueing, Guiding; Belief vs. Doubt; Allow for Ambiguity, Generalization and Dreams; From Monologue to Dialogue" [V] (Appreciative Interviews). The discipline of the interviewer: "Let the interviewee tell his/her story, please don't tell yours or give your opinion about their experiences. Take good notes and be listening for great quotes and stories. Be genuinely curious about their experiences, thoughts and feelings. Some people will take longer to think about their answers – allow for silence" [V]. Possible probe questions include "Tell me more"; "Why do you feel that way?"; "Why was that important to you?"; "What was your contribution?"; "What was the organization doing that helped you do this?"; "What do think was really making it work?"; "How has it changed you?" [V] (Appreciative Interviews). Pointer to lineage: "Appreciative Interviews are part of a larger process called Appreciative Inquiry (AI)" with reference to `appreciativeinquiry.case.edu` [BT] (Appreciative Interviews).

**Discovery & Action Dialogues (DADs).** Three named purposes: "Engage everyone in short, lively conversations to discover the existing solutions they already know and to create new ideas to eliminate and prevent the target problem"; "Identify volunteers among this group to experiment with solutions and ideas"; "Provide the facilitators and participants the opportunity to listen to each other and identify barriers to action or change" [V] (Discovery & Action Dialogues). Standard DAD questions: "What do you know/think about ________?"; "What do YOU do about ______________?"; "What keeps you from doing that all the time?"; "Who/Where have you seen overcome those barriers?"; "What other ideas do you have about removing barriers? (or supporting desired behavior)"; "What has to happen next to make that happen?"; "Who will do what when next?" [V]. Pointer to lineage: "The D&A Dialogue process is one developed as part of Plexus Institute's work with Positive Deviance that we applied to quality and safety problems in hospitals" with pointers to `plexusinstitute.org` and `positivedeviance.org` [BT] (Discovery & Action Dialogues).

### Question-asking and assumption-surfacing structures

**5 Whys.** "A question-asking technique used to explore the cause-and-effect relationships underlying a particular problem. The original goal of the technique was to determine the root cause of a defect or problem as part of a quality improvement effort. However, this process can be used to go deeper to explore questions related to purpose rather than problems" [V] (5 Whys). Operationally: "Invite participants to pair up… Each participant will get a turn being the question asker and the responder. The role of the question asker is to be an active listener, pose the question and ask 'WHY?' at least five times after hearing the answer or a variation such as 'Why is that important? And why do you care about that?'" [V]. The handbook explicitly relaxes the count: "you don't need to stop after 5 Whys. Keep going until you feel you've pared away enough layers to get at a fundamental truth" [V] (5 Whys).

**Wicked Questions.** "Wicked questions are used to expose the assumptions that we hold about an issue or situation. Articulating these assumptions provides an opportunity to see the patterns of thought and surface the differences in a group" [V] (Wicked Questions). The discriminator from a trick question: "A question is 'wicked' if there is an embedded paradox or tension in the question. A wicked question is not a trick question. With a trick question, someone knows the answer. Wicked questions do not have obvious answers. Their value lies in their capacity to open up options, inquiry and bring to the surface the fundamental issues that need to be addressed" [V] (Wicked Questions). Two worked examples: "How can we sustain quality standards across the system while allowing for local innovation?" and "How can we maintain top down discipline needed for safety and level the playing field for bottom-up creativity?" — paired against a counter-example: "How can we succeed when the guys at headquarters are such bozos?" (which is not wicked because it is a complaint, not a paradox) [V] (Wicked Questions).

**Q Storming.** "Diana Whitney, one of the originators of Appreciative Inquiry, says: 'Questions are fateful!'" [BT — Diana Whitney] (Q Storming). Operationally: post-it-note brainstorm of "courageous and provocative questions as well as 'silly' and 'dumb' questions"; questions are categorised, prioritised (e.g. dot voting), or read aloud in patterns [V] (Q Storming). Suggested prompts: "What questions would you most like to walk out of here having answered? What's the question that, if you could answer, would make it possible for you to move forward or get unstuck? What questions do we need to address in order to make the choices we need to make? What questions need to be resolved before we can start on this initiative?" [V]. Lineage: "The Q-Storming process was originally developed by Marilee G. Adams" with pointer to `inquiryinstitute.com/resources/q-storming/` [BT] (Q Storming).

**TRIZ.** A pared-down adaptation of the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving: "TRIZ is 'a problem-solving, analysis and forecasting tool derived from the study of patterns of invention. It was developed by the Soviet inventor and science fiction author Genrich Altshuller and his colleagues in the 1940s.'… As a Liberating Structure, we're only going to use one piece of the TRIZ approach" [V] (TRIZ) [BT — Altshuller]. The single move named: "Design a comprehensive system that makes it absolutely impossible to get that result. What policies, practices, and ways of operating would make it 100% certain there is no way any of the things you want can happen. Does the system you've designed have anything in common with the current state of affairs? What would it take to eliminate similarities between the current system and the adverse system you designed?" [V] (TRIZ).

### Decision-tools and prioritisation structures

**25-to-10.** A wisdom-of-the-crowd rating process. "Distribute file cards to everyone. Pose a question. Ask everyone to think about it and then write their best idea clearly on the card. No names" [V] (25-to-10). Five rounds of milling, trading, and rating ideas on a 1-5 scale; "By round five, each index card should have five ratings. Add them up. What is the score?" [V]. The countdown surfaces the high-scoring ideas: "Does anyone have a card with a score of 25… 24… 23… until you get a 'yes.' Ask that person to read their card out loud" [V]. An explicit scope caveat — the handbook does not present 25-to-10 as a decision rule: "This is not recommended to make a decision. It's a way to get a sense of the wisdom of the crowd" [V] (25-to-10).

**Mini Constellations.** A spatial-positioning method. "Place an object on the floor to represent an idea, choice, move, etc. Ask participants to place themselves somewhere in the room that represents to what degree they feel positively about the idea" [V] (Mini Constellations). Participants speak from their distance-position as a "role," not as a personal view: "this way of giving the instruction invites them to take the 'role' of someone with that perspective and not simply be representing a personal view" [V]. The handbook flags lineage: "Constellations is a process rooted in family therapy. This is a very small exercise from a much more comprehensive body of work" [BT — family-therapy constellations] (Mini Constellations).

**15% Solutions.** "Most people have about 15-percent control over their work situations. The other 85 percent rests in the broader context, shaped by the general structures, systems, events and culture in which they operate" [V] (15% Solutions). Quotes Gareth Morgan in *The Globe and Mail*: "The challenge rests in finding ways of creating transformational change incrementally: By encouraging people to mobilize small but significant '15-percent initiatives' that can snowball in their effects" [V] (15% Solutions) [BT — Gareth Morgan]. The democratising framing: "It doesn't matter if you're a General or an enlisted soldier, a Senior Executive or a member of the team. You still have only your 15 percent" [V]. The diagnostic question: "Where do you have freedom to act? What's in your 15%?" [V] (15% Solutions).

**Simple Rules.** "Patterns are generated by simple rules. These are the 'constraints' (like the rules of Jazz) that create the space for creativity but, at the same time, describe the critical elements that make something work" [V] (Simple Rules). Five named categories: "How To rules, Boundary Rules, Priority Rules, Timing Rules, and Exit Rules" [V]. The diagnostic move: "What simple rules would you give the design team? What are the few key things that MUST be included in the design? What is really mandatory?" — then "find a partner and 'test' each of the rules on the list. What would happen if you left that rule out? Is this really required versus just an artifact of what we've been doing all along?" [V] (Simple Rules).

**Six Words.** A compression exercise inspired by an attributed Hemingway anecdote ("It's said that Hemingway was once challenged to write a whole novel in just six words. His novel: 'For sale: baby shoes, never worn.'") [V] (Six Words) [BT — Hemingway anecdote; Larry Smith/SMITH Magazine]. The handbook borrows criteria from the Mulago Foundation for six-word mission statements: "Less than 8 words; Include a verb; Include a target population; Describes an outcome you can measure" [V] (Six Words) [BT — Mulago Foundation]. Examples: "Save African kids' lives"; "Get African families out of extreme poverty"; "Save island species from extinction" [V]. The framing: "This is especially useful if you want/need to talk about particularly mushy stuff like missions, vision, purpose, or strategy - all the things that can sound like gobblety-gook, blah blah blah! Taking some of the seriousness out of these things can actually help people get to the core of an important idea" [V] (Six Words).

**Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix.** A complexity-classification tool: "Ralph Stacey created one way to think about this distinction by distinguishing two axes – the degree to which a group is certain about what's going on and the degree to which there is agreement about what to do or about the meaning of different aspects of the situation" [V] (Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix) [BT — Ralph Stacey]. Borrows Brenda Zimmerman's recipe/airplane/child distinction: "Brenda Zimmerman suggests that a recipe is simple; building an airplane is complicated. There are lots of parts and formulas but if you understand aerodynamics you can pretty much put one together and be pretty certain it will fly. But raising a child is complex" [V] (Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix) [BT — Brenda Zimmerman]. The reason the matrix matters: "Many of our strategies and heuristics for working on problems are designed for simple or complicated situations. These strategies don't work well when things are complex because the situation is emergent and unpredictable" [V].

**Portfolio Planning.** Brenda Zimmerman's eco-cycle framing applied to project portfolios: "Brenda Zimmerman uses forest fires to describe different phases of an eco-cycle. A key understanding is that creative destruction is critical to create space for renewal" [V] (Portfolio Planning) [BT — Brenda Zimmerman]. Operationally: "invite participants to work in small groups to talk about where different activities might fit in a portfolio of projects that could be placed in different parts of the eco-cycle"; a wall-size version with post-it activities is named as a variation [V].

### Group-storytelling and dialogue structures

**After Action Debrief: What, So What, Now What?** Three sequential questions, "in the following order" [V] (After Action Debrief): "WHAT? What is emerging here? What data stands out? What actually happened? SO WHAT? What do the data imply? Do you see a pattern? Does it make a difference? NOW WHAT? What action may help us move forward? Who else should be here?" [V]. The handbook attributes the question structure to Center for Creative Leadership: "What? So What? Now What? is an exercise included many of the Center for Creative Leadership's creative thinking and leadership programs" [BT — Center for Creative Leadership] (After Action Debrief).

**Conversation Café Dialogue.** Operates with named *Café Agreements* (Open-mindedness, Acceptance, Curiosity, Discovery, Sincerity, Brevity) and a *talking object* [V] (Conversation Café Dialogue). Three rounds plus a final round: Round 1, "Pass around the talking object; each person speaks briefly to the topic, no feedback or response… about a paragraph or two from each person"; Round 2, "each person deepens his/her own comments or speaks to what has meaning now… the equivalent of a page from each person"; Dialogue, "Open, spirited conversation. Use talking object. Suggest that whomever wishes to speak just hold out their hand to receive the object"; Final Round, "each person says briefly what challenged, touched or inspired them" [V] (Conversation Café Dialogue). Pointer to lineage: `conversationcafe.org` [BT].

**World Café.** A large-group dialogue: "small round tables covered with a checkered tablecloth, butcher block paper, colored pens, a vase of flowers, and optional 'talking stick' item. There should be four chairs at each table" [V] (World Café). "Three or more twenty minute rounds of conversation… each member of the group moves to a different new table. They may or may not choose to leave one person as the 'table host' for the next round, who welcomes the next group and briefly fills them in on what happened in the previous round" [V]. Each round has a question; "The same questions can be used for more than one round, or they can be built upon each other to focus the conversation or guide its direction" [V]. Harvest happens either "after the small groups and/or in between rounds… These results are reflected visually in a variety of ways often using graphic recorders in the front of the room" [V] (World Café). Pointer to lineage: `theworldcafe.com/method.html` [BT].

**Fishbowl.** "Create a circle of chairs in the center of a larger circle. 5-6 is a good number… Invite a small group of people that have direct experience with the challenge into the small circle of chairs at the center. Ask this group to talk about the challenge together, sharing stories of their direct experience and insights as they might do if they were sitting in a coffee shop or at dinner together. They talk to each other, NOT the audience" [V] (Fishbowl). The audience role is to "ask questions and share their insights about the conversation while those in the center circle just listen"; then "invite the group to dialogue with each other between the two circles" [V] (Fishbowl). Debrief questions: "What did you hear that surprised you? How has your perspective on the issue changed? What questions are still open for you?" [V].

**Shift & Share.** "A good way to provide opportunities to hear experience stories, research results, or project plans from multiple groups while avoiding linear, didactic reports" [V] (Shift & Share). Teams set up locations in the room; participants travel between teams, attending each as a small group rather than a serial keynote.

**Just In Time Topics.** A self-organising agenda inspired by Open Space Technology: "If possible, start with everyone in a circle… Invite participants to write down a topic – a question, story, or problem – that they would like to spend time (usually 45-90 minutes) talking about with others" [V] (Just In Time Topics). Topics are posted, hosted, and chosen by participants. The handbook surfaces two named "laws" used in the practice: "Whoever comes are the right people. Don't worry about who or how many – it can even be valuable to spend the hour reflecting on the topic by yourself" and "The law of two feet. If you're not contributing or getting value, use your feet to go somewhere else" [V] (Just In Time Topics). Lineage: "This process is inspired by a larger process called OPEN SPACE" with pointer to `openspaceworld.org` [BT].

### Scenario-building and network structures

**Participatory Scenarios.** A critique of expert-only scenario planning: "Many companies develop scenarios to support strategic planning. These scenarios are often created by 'experts' or small teams. As a result, many companies discover that lack of ownership on the part of the whole community means that the scenarios don't really play a part in decision making" [V] (Participatory Scenarios). The alternative: a five-step community-driven scenario build. "Identify a set of drivers that can be described along a dimension"; "Pick any 2 of the dimensions and set them up on a matrix"; "Fill in the resulting landscape… characteristics that result from the combination, consequences, key questions raised, or potential opportunities/challenges"; "Develop a 'story' that describes the scenario in a way that makes it come alive and give it a name (for example, in the above matrix 'Virtually Vanilla')"; "Create a different combination and repeat - as many times as you can!" [V] (Participatory Scenarios).

**Network Weaving.** "Invite participants to draw a 'map' of the key network connections around a specific project, goal or initiative" [V] (Network Weaving). Three named analytic moves after maps are drawn: "Closing Triangles: Which two individuals that you know don't know each other? Which ones of those pairs would benefit from knowing each other? What do they have in common?"; "Integrating clusters: What clusters do you notice? Do they have much overlap? How could you initiate introductions among the clusters?"; "Expanding the periphery: Are there as many nodes in the periphery as in the core? If not, how might you expand the network's periphery? What types of individuals, organizations or perspectives are missing or underrepresented (gender, race/ethnicity, class, neighborhoods, etc) and how might you include these individuals to the network?" [V] (Network Weaving).

### Levels-of-Accountability ladder

A sectional summary appears on its own page as a stair-step list rather than a paragraph essay. The handbook names seven labelled levels (paired with disposition phrases). Reading the source page from top to bottom: "Take Action and Learn — Make it happen, renewal"; "Find Solutions — 'This can work'"; "Accept ownership — 'It starts with me'"; "Acknowledge situation — 'I'm a piece of the puzzle'"; "Wait 'n Hope — 'Somebody should do something'"; "Blame self — 'It's my fault,' guilt"; "Blame others — 'It's their fault'"; with a bottom layer "Denial — 'Woe is me'" and "'This has nothing to do with me.'" The top band carries the labels *Learn & Grow* and *Empowered*; the bottom band carries the labels *Protect & Defend* and *Disempowered* [V] (Levels of Accountability). The page closes with one diagnostic question: "What do we need to do to work 'above the line?'" [V]. The handbook does not attribute the ladder to a specific author within its body text.

### Improv-derived and embodied structures

**Improv Games.** Three named exercises are surfaced with one-paragraph descriptions: "One Word at a Time Story" ("All players sit in a circle. We are going to tell a story one word at a time. Each player provides one word of a sentence. The end of a sentence can be indicates by a player saying 'period,' although that is not necessary"); "Yes And Exercise" ("In Yes And the players are constantly saying, 'yes and.'… The players must always have the 'yes and' at the beginning of their sentence. This seems contrived and it is. It is remarkable how much easier it is to notice players that insist on controlling the scene. They cannot bring themselves to accept the offer. The most common response is, 'yes and but'"); and "Coffee Filters" ("Give everyone a coffee filter. Invite them to play with it… What else can you do with it? What do you notice about what happens?") [V] (Improv Games). Pointer to lineage: Tom Sparough, "The Space Painter!" `spacepainter.com` [BT] (Improv Games).

## Part III: "Seeing complexity" exercises and "Random Thoughts" framing-aids

The handbook closes the activity catalogue with two short groupings that supplement the named structures.

### Three complexity-seeing exercises

**Complex Systems Game: How Many Ways?** Lunch bags filled with 12–25 random objects; groups invent classification schemes; "Invite groups to look at each other's results and guess what criteria they used to organize the objects" [V] (Complex Systems Game). The debrief framing makes the concept explicit: "an opportunity to talk about how people can 'see' very different things based on their experience, their initial ideas, the makeup of their group, and a host of other factors" [V] (Complex Systems Game). Debrief prompts: "Is there a 'right' answer? Is there a wrong answer? What influences how a group gets to their solution? What assumptions did your group make about the objects that influenced how you grouped them?" [V].

**Tic Tac Toe.** A two-person no-talking, no-touching air-game: "Play a game of Tic Tac Toe in the air. Play several games. Now, play a 4 X 4 game of Tic Tac Toe in the air. Most people can play 3 x 3 after a bit. But most people find that the 4 x 4 version is impossible" [V] (Tic Tac Toe). The framing claim: "We spend a lot of time in meetings doing the equivalent of playing 4 x 4 tic tac toe in the air. We THINK that we are all talking about the same things, on the same page, having the same assumptions, or defining things the same way. But we rarely take the time necessary, especially when we're talking about complex problems and situations and organizations, to make explicit all the things where we need a common understanding in order to make progress" [V] (Tic Tac Toe).

**A Place in the System.** A participants-stand-and-position exercise. "Find one person and choose them as Person A and another person as Person B… Instruct them to 'get between' Person A and Person B" — the group mills until a stable configuration emerges; then "Instruct them to get equidistant from Person A and Person B" [V] (A Place in the System). The framing: "This exercise gives an opportunity to talk about what it's like when you are not 'in control' of factors that are important to your plans. What happens when everything is 'moving?'" [V] (A Place in the System).

### "Random Thoughts" framing-aids

The handbook gathers five facilitator-craft micro-techniques as a coda:

- **Waving Hands versus Clapping** — "Some groups find that having a norm of waving hands in the air is nicer than clapping. It makes the point without breaking the mood" [V] (Waving Hands versus Clapping).
- **Talking Objects** — described as a culturally-respectful alternative to a talking stick: "Many traditions include ceremonies and other processes that use a Talking Stick… so it can be more comfortable to use a 'talking object' instead." The handbook attributes the talking-stick tradition to Native American practice as described by Dr. Locust at the American Research and Training Center in Tucson, Arizona, including a quoted passage: "The talking stick has been used for centuries by many Indian tribes as a means of just and impartial hearing… Whoever holds the talking stick has within his hands the power of words. Only he can speak while he holds the stick, and the other council members must remain silent. The eagle feather tied to the stick gives him the courage and wisdom to speak truthfully and wisely. The rabbit fur on the end of the stick, reminds him that his words must come from his heart" [V] (Talking Objects) [BT — Dr. Locust, 1998].
- **Music & Poetry** — "If you really want to create the conditions for creativity and out-of-the-box thinking, add a bit of poetry & music to the mix" [V] (Music & Poetry).
- **Tingsha Bells** — A practical solution to a recurring problem: "Liberating Structures require participants to move in and out of groups frequently. You need a way to call them back – from groups, from the hall, from outside. YELLING is abrasive. Tapping on a microphone is grating. Find another way" [V] (Tingsha Bells). Suggests Tingsha bells "though the TSA folks may want to see what they are in your luggage!" — and notes "Kid's xylophones also work" [V].
- **Take 20 Seconds (Pause and look at your shoes)** — "When it is more important to create a real conversation than it is to provide all the 'right' answers, which is almost always, facilitators can do this by asking the assembled group good questions and then WAITING for people in the group to answer" [V] (Take 20 Seconds). The handbook offers a striking calibration figure: "20 seconds of silence in a group can feel like a very long time. On average, facilitators begin speaking after six seconds. So, increase your tolerance of loooong pauses" [V] (Take 20 Seconds). The mechanism is named: "During these long pauses people in the group are often formulating their answers and working up the courage to respond. If you start talking too soon, you truncate that process and communicate, unwittingly, that you're the one with the answers" [V]. The physical move: "look down at your shoes, this disrupts your eye contact with members of the group, signals that this is time for reflection and thinking and takes a little of the pressure to begin talking off you" [V] (Take 20 Seconds).

## Part IV: Origins and the pattern-language framing

### Lineage of the term

The handbook attributes the term *liberating structures* to William Torbert: "The idea of liberating structures was first introduced by William Torbert in *The Power of Balance: Transforming Self, Society, and Scientific Inquiry* (Sage, 1991). Bill's interest in an integral approach to leadership and action inquiry led him to explore the notion of a form of organizational structure that gives guidance to people but in such a way that they develop skills to guide themselves. He developed a theory of power that generates productivity, justice, and inquiry and a theory of liberating structure through which organizations can create continual quality improvement" [V] (Origins) [BT — Torbert 1991].

Edward de Bono is named as a contemporaneous source for the same distinction. The handbook quotes de Bono's *Teaching Thinking* (Penguin, 1991): "We can distinguish between restricting structures and liberating structures. Tools are liberating structures. With the proper tools students will surprise themselves with ideas that they have not had before" [V] (Origins) [BT — de Bono 1991].

The connection to process design is attributed to the field of facilitators and organisational-development practitioners working with large-group methods over the preceding 20 years. Dick Axelrod is named: "In his book, *Terms of Engagement: New Ways of Leading and Changing Organizations* (2nd ed., Berrett-Koehler, 2010), Dick Axelrod describes essential principles that characterize popular large-group methods such as Open Space, Appreciative Inquiry, the Conference Model, and others. These include widening the circle of involvement, connecting people to each other and other ideas, creating communities for action, and practicing democratic principles" [V] (Origins) [BT — Axelrod 2010].

The handbook makes its own framing claim about what these methods share: "we began to recognize the loose-tight quality of some of the dynamics that made them work" [V] (Origins). And it offers a worked example of the kind of structure these methods carry: "Open Space Technology is guided by four principles, one law, and a set of common practices. With these, any group can self organize around any topic. These kinds of comprehensive change strategies typically play out over several days. Some include multiple sessions that take place weeks or months apart and are often led by teams of consultants" [V] (Origins).

### The pattern-language framing

The closing essay grounds the catalogue in Christopher Alexander's pattern-language work: "What we need is a pattern language for talking about these engagement methods in ways that are accessible. Christopher Alexander (*A Pattern Language*, Oxford U, 1977) developed the idea of a pattern language in the context of architecture and community environments to identify patterns that work in social spaces" [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement) [BT — Alexander 1977]. The handbook gives Alexander's *intimacy gradient* as a worked example: "In any building, house, or office building, people experience a gradient of settings that have different degrees of intimacy. A bedroom is the most intimate and a study is less so. A common area or kitchen is more public. The front porch or entrance is the most public of all. People feel and work best when these patterns are present and recognizable in their social space. Talking about and using the vocabulary of these patterns allows designers and community members, planners and architects to think and talk about the implications of different choices" [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement).

Peter and Trudy Johnson-Lenz are named as a complementary lineage: "Peter and Trudy Johnson-Lenz talk about rhythms, boundaries, and containers as primitives: universal, fundamental patterns from which all life is built, including our social life. They suggest that our face-to-face contacts often occur in regular rhythms. Boundaries of many sorts pattern when and where we connect and when and where we do not. Physical and social containers frame and hold our meetings. The skillful use of these tools is the critical capacity of experienced group facilitators" [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement) [BT — Peter and Trudy Johnson-Lenz].

The synthesis the handbook lands on:

> "As a pattern language for engagement, liberating structures give us multiple options for each of these primitives: the rhythm/timing of each round of interaction, the boundaries of group size and inclusion, physical containers like space and room set-up, and conceptual containers created by the way a question is phrased. All liberating structures are made up of these simple sets of components that can be combined in dozens of different ways."
> (A Pattern Language for Engagement)

The closing essay also names the *half-life* problem that motivated the handbook's existence: "The half-life of the energy and commitment to new ways of being after these events can be short when participants return to their organizations and fall back into default ways of meeting. Frequently, the changes are not sustained. So, how can we extend that half-life? How can we make the enlivening experience that characterizes these energetic events available every day? How can we put the power to host and facilitate high engagement in the hands of everyone in the organization?" [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement).

## Key statistics, counts, and timing prescriptions (from the source)

| Item | Value | Citation |
|---|---|---|
| Handbook page count | 60 pages | structure line |
| Number of named LS interaction designs in the catalogue | ~32 (Knee-to-Knee through Improv Games; plus three "Seeing Complexity" exercises and five "Random Thoughts" framing-aids) | [V] Table of Contents and section pages |
| Recommended café-table seating | "4-tops (that seat 4-6 people)" | [V] (Choosing Space) |
| 5 Whys minimum repetition | "at least five times"; explicitly relaxable beyond five | [V] (5 Whys) |
| 1-2-4-Whole Group small-group size | "4-6. More than six is too big to give everyone a chance to talk" | [V] (1-2-4-Whole Group) |
| Impromptu Speed Networking rounds | "Three 'rounds' are usually good" | [V] (Impromptu Speed Networking) |
| Impromptu Speed Networking air-time in 30-min session | "12-15 minutes of air time – no matter how many participants" | [V] (Impromptu Speed Networking) |
| Take a Panel individual work time | "about 20 minutes"; small-group share "about 4 minutes each" | [V] (Take A Panel) |
| Generative questions for Take a Panel | "8-10 generative questions" | [V] (Take A Panel) |
| Wise Crowds group size | "5-6 people" | [V] (Wise Crowds) |
| Wise Crowds phase timings | Client describes ~10 minutes; team converses with client's back turned ≥15 minutes; client rejoins for ~15 minutes | [V] (Wise Crowds) |
| Troika Consulting round length | "10 minutes" per focal person; three rounds | [V] (Troika Consulting) |
| Conversation Café small group size | "6-8 is a good number" | [V] (Conversation Café Dialogue) |
| World Café table size | "four chairs at each table" | [V] (World Café) |
| World Café round structure | "three or more twenty minute rounds" | [V] (World Café) |
| 25-to-10 rating rounds | "Repeat five times, in five rounds" | [V] (25-to-10) |
| 25-to-10 rating scale | 1 to 5 | [V] (25-to-10) |
| Fishbowl inner-circle size | "5-6 is a good number" | [V] (Fishbowl) |
| 15% Solutions framing percentage | "about 15-percent control" personally; "the other 85 percent rests in the broader context" | [V] (15% Solutions) |
| Six Words criteria (per Mulago Foundation) | <8 words; verb; target population; measurable outcome | [V] (Six Words) [BT] |
| Just In Time Topics conversation length | "usually 45-90 minutes" | [V] (Just In Time Topics) |
| Tic Tac Toe difficulty threshold | 3×3 air play tractable; 4×4 air play "impossible" for most | [V] (Tic Tac Toe) |
| Facilitator wait time after a question (average vs prescribed) | average: "after six seconds"; prescribed minimum: "at least 20 seconds" | [V] (Take 20 Seconds) |
| Categories of simple rules named | 5 — "How To rules, Boundary Rules, Priority Rules, Timing Rules, and Exit Rules" | [V] (Simple Rules) |
| What-So-What-Now-What questions | 3 sequential questions in the named order | [V] (After Action Debrief) |
| Pattern-language primitives named for LS | 4 — rhythm/timing, boundaries (group size and inclusion), physical containers (space and room set-up), conceptual containers (question phrasing) | [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement) |
| Levels of Accountability levels named | 7 ladder rungs from "Take Action and Learn" to "Blame others"; plus a "Denial" / "'This has nothing to do with me'" bottom layer | [V] (Levels of Accountability) |

## Connections the author makes in the text

The handbook is a compilation source and is dense with attributions. Listed in source order of first appearance:

- **Peter Block.** Cited approvingly for "the power of small groups sitting knee-to-knee" with pointer to `asmallgroup.net` [BT] (Knee-to-Knee Conversation).
- **Plexus Institute / Positive Deviance.** Origin lineage for Discovery & Action Dialogues, "applied to quality and safety problems in hospitals"; pointers to `plexusinstitute.org` and `positivedeviance.org` [BT] (Discovery & Action Dialogues).
- **Genrich Altshuller.** Soviet inventor and science-fiction author credited as TRIZ originator in the 1940s; "the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving" [BT] (TRIZ); pointer to `triz.co.uk`.
- **Ralph Stacey.** Cited as the originator of the Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix's two-axis framing [BT] (Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix).
- **Brenda Zimmerman.** Cited for the recipe/airplane/child distinction and for the forest-fire eco-cycle framing of project portfolios [BT] (Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix; Portfolio Planning).
- **Gareth Morgan.** Quoted from *The Globe and Mail* on 15-percent initiatives [BT] (15% Solutions).
- **Diana Whitney.** Named as "one of the originators of Appreciative Inquiry" and quoted ("Questions are fateful!") [BT] (Q Storming; also implicit in Appreciative Interviews via the pointer to `appreciativeinquiry.case.edu`).
- **Marilee G. Adams.** Credited as the developer of Q-Storming; pointer to `inquiryinstitute.com/resources/q-storming/` [BT] (Q Storming).
- **Mulago Foundation.** Source for the four Six-Words mission-statement criteria [BT] (Six Words).
- **Larry Smith / SMITH Magazine.** Source for the "Six-Word Memoir" framing [BT] (Six Words).
- **Hemingway.** Anecdotal source for "For sale: baby shoes, never worn." [BT — anecdotal attribution] (Six Words).
- **Center for Creative Leadership.** Source for the What-So-What-Now-What framing [BT] (After Action Debrief).
- **Tom Sparough ("The Space Painter").** Source for the Coffee Filters improv exercise; pointer to `spacepainter.com` [BT] (Improv Games).
- **Open Space Technology / openspaceworld.org.** Larger process from which Just In Time Topics draws its self-organising frame; the handbook names four-principles, one-law, common-practices structure [BT] (Just In Time Topics; Origins).
- **Conversation Café.** Process-lineage pointer for Conversation Café Dialogue at `conversationcafe.org` [BT] (Conversation Café Dialogue).
- **World Café.** Process-lineage pointer at `theworldcafe.com/method.html` [BT] (World Café).
- **Appreciative Inquiry (AI).** Larger process behind Appreciative Interviews; pointer to `appreciativeinquiry.case.edu` [BT] (Appreciative Interviews).
- **Family-therapy Constellations.** Larger body of work behind Mini Constellations; pointer to `familyconstellationwork.net` [BT] (Mini Constellations).
- **Dr. Locust (American Research and Training Center, Tucson, Arizona).** 1998 source for the Native American talking-stick passage [BT] (Talking Objects).
- **The Grove.** Source for portfolio-planning templates [BT] (Portfolio Planning); pointer to `grove.com`.
- **William Torbert.** *The Power of Balance: Transforming Self, Society, and Scientific Inquiry* (Sage, 1991), credited as the originator of the term *liberating structures* [BT] (Origins).
- **Edward de Bono.** *Teaching Thinking* (Penguin, 1991), quoted on the restricting-vs-liberating-structures distinction [BT] (Origins).
- **Dick Axelrod.** *Terms of Engagement: New Ways of Leading and Changing Organizations* (2nd ed., Berrett-Koehler, 2010), cited as a contemporary articulation of the shared principles of large-group methods [BT] (Origins).
- **Christopher Alexander.** *A Pattern Language* (Oxford U, 1977), grounding source for the pattern-language framing and the *intimacy gradient* worked example [BT] (A Pattern Language for Engagement).
- **Peter and Trudy Johnson-Lenz.** Cited for "rhythms, boundaries, and containers as primitives" [BT] (A Pattern Language for Engagement).

## Positions the author explicitly frames against

- *Buy-in as a goal.* "Buy-in is the exact opposite [of ownership]… Aiming for buy-in creates lukewarm, pallid implementation and mediocre results" and "Anytime you or someone around you thinks or talks about buy-in, beware!" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in).
- *Designing for buy-in in intractable socio-technical systems.* "When it comes to solving intractable socio-technical behavioral problems in systems the notion of buy-in is just not useful – people in the system need to own the new behaviors" [V] (Ownership versus Buy-in).
- *Large tables in conference centres for "juicy conversation".* "The round tables typically available in most conference centers and hotels are too large for juicy conversation because people end up too far apart and have to yell across to be heard in a full room" [V] (Choosing Space).
- *Fluorescent or artificial lighting for all-day events.* "Lighting is critical and most people wilt when meeting all day in artificial – especially fluorescent - light" [V] (Aesthetics Are Important).
- *Agendas as the default planning artefact.* "Try using a storyboard instead of an agenda…" — the storyboard's value is that it "also includes and illustrates something about what the participants will experience" [V] (Storyboard Design and Planning).
- *Designing without "the people most involved".* "When searching in your organization or community for behavior and solutions that are unusually powerful and effective (positively deviant), it helps to broaden your conversations to include the people most involved" [V] (Nothing About Me Without Me).
- *Window-dressing invitations.* "Many people have been to too many meetings where they were invited as window dressing without the opportunity to contribute, so they may be suspicious" [V] (Engage the Unusual Suspects).
- *Listening to any one voice for more than 5 minutes.* Cited as "draining/deadening the energy in the meeting" [V] (Celebrity Interviews).
- *Q-A-Q-A patterns reinforcing "central tendency".* "Avoids getting into a Q A Q A Q A Q A pattern that tends to reinforce the 'central tendency' versus the distributed intelligence of the group" [V] (Taking Questions).
- *Report-outs.* "'Report Outs' from breakout sessions are BORING!!!… most of the time, we don't really need to hear serial summaries of all the discussions. And, it typically drains the life out of whatever conversation that took place in the small group" [V] (A Note about Report Outs).
- *Wise Crowds as "tell-the-client-what-to-do".* "The Consulting Team's job is not to decide 'this is what The Client ought to do'" [V] (Wise Crowds).
- *Expert-only strategic scenario planning.* "These scenarios are often created by 'experts' or small teams. As a result, many companies discover that lack of ownership on the part of the whole community means that the scenarios don't really play a part in decision making" [V] (Participatory Scenarios).
- *Combining topics in Just In Time Topics.* "Encourage participants to resist the temptation to combine topics (since everything is related) because smaller group discussions are more satisfying" [V] (Just In Time Topics).
- *Trick questions framed as wicked ones.* "A wicked question is not a trick question. With a trick question, someone knows the answer. Wicked questions do not have obvious answers" [V] (Wicked Questions). And a counter-example named directly as not wicked: "How can we succeed when the guys at headquarters are such bozos?" [V].
- *25-to-10 as a decision rule.* "This is not recommended to make a decision. It's a way to get a sense of the wisdom of the crowd" [V] (25-to-10).
- *Designers and facilitators over-specifying constraints.* "Debrief the process to explore the extent to which we 'over' versus 'under' specify constraints" [V] (Simple Rules) — note the phrasing acknowledges both failure modes but the surrounding text is concerned with the over-specification failure mode (rules retained "just an artifact of what we've been doing all along").
- *Strategies designed for simple or complicated problems applied to complex ones.* "Many of our strategies and heuristics for working on problems are designed for simple or complicated situations. These strategies don't work well when things are complex because the situation is emergent and unpredictable" [V] (Agreement/Uncertainty Matrix).
- *Yelling or microphone-tapping to recall participants.* "YELLING is abrasive. Tapping on a microphone is grating. Find another way" [V] (Tingsha Bells).
- *Facilitator speaking before the group does.* "On average, facilitators begin speaking after six seconds" — and the prescription is to wait at least 20 [V] (Take 20 Seconds); "If you start talking too soon, you truncate that process and communicate, unwittingly, that you're the one with the answers" [V].
- *Letting LS events fade after the room empties.* "The half-life of the energy and commitment to new ways of being after these events can be short when participants return to their organizations and fall back into default ways of meeting. Frequently, the changes are not sustained" [V] (A Pattern Language for Engagement).

## Citation and source-integrity notes

The source is a 60-page born-digital-style PDF acquired from `https://www.grassrootsgrantmakers.org/wp-content/uploads/Engaging-Everyone-31.pdf` (SHA-256: `df336e2f346ffea3377078798c0f684f755daa75c846c1f8a8476b7274928364`). Conversion to markdown used `pymupdf4llm` 0.0.30, producing approximately 2,767 lines of markdown with page-boundary markers (`2`–`60`) preserved as inline numerals. Heading hierarchy is preserved: each named LS structure appears as a `#####` bold-bracketed heading; the introduction and front-matter sections (Ownership versus Buy-in, Choosing Space, etc.) use the same pattern.

Citation style chosen: `(Section, "Sub-section name")`. The handbook has no chapter numbering; each named structure is its own section header. Page numbers exist in the source but each page boundary often interrupts a section mid-paragraph, making sub-section names the more useful locator. Page numbers are noted only where the handbook itself uses them (e.g. the Table of Contents lists page numbers per section).

Full source coverage: all 60 pages were read. No partial-coverage caveat applies. The licence statement on the source's copyright/intro page reads "Engaging Everyone with Liberating Structures Handbook by Group Jazz is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported License. Based on a work at www.groupjazz.com" and is verbatim in the converted markdown. The compilers' email is included in the front-matter: "Please feel free to copy and share this material. We'd also love to hear about your 'liberating structures' designs and experience so let us know! lisa@groupjazz.com" [V] (front matter), which is consistent with the NC-SA licence: copying and sharing are licensed and encouraged; commercial use is not.

A small number of typographical artefacts in the source are preserved as found in the deep ref without "correction": the Table of Contents entry "DISCOVERTY & ACTION DIALOGUES" (the body header uses the correct spelling "DISCOVERY & ACTION DIALOGUES"); the "25-T0-10" zero-vs-letter-O in the Table of Contents (the body header is "25–TO-10"); the inconsistent spelling "Liberating Structures" capitalisation across sections. These do not warrant strip from the deep ref and are not load-bearing for any cited claim.
